home.page_title: Parth Parmar | Senior Software Engineer, ML Infrastructure
home.description: Parth Parmar is a senior software engineer focused on ML infrastructure, platform engineering, and reliable production systems.
hero_lead: I’m a software engineer focused on building infrastructure for large-scale AI systems. My work sits at the intersection of distributed systems and machine learning infrastructure, where the goal is not only to build powerful models but to make them reliable, scalable, and usable in real-world products.

At Amazon Ads, I’ve worked on systems supporting LLM-powered products, including agent infrastructure, synthetic data generation pipelines for post-training, and evaluation platforms that help measure and improve model performance at scale.

I’m particularly interested in the engineering challenges that arise when machine learning systems move from research to production. Many of the hardest problems in AI today are not just about better models, but about building the surrounding infrastructure — data pipelines, evaluation systems, and distributed platforms that allow these models to operate reliably. This website is a place where I share technical writing, experiments, and notes on topics like ML infrastructure, LLM systems, and scalable AI platforms.

blog.description: Blog by Parth Parmar covering ML infrastructure, platform engineering, and reliable systems.
blog.heading: Notes on ML infrastructure, platform work, and reliable systems.
blog.lead: Short writing on the kinds of engineering problems I want to spend more time on.

resume.description: Resume for Parth Parmar covering software engineering, platform work, and ML infrastructure direction.
resume.heading: Parth Parmar
resume.lead: Software engineer focused on machine learning infrastructure and large-scale distributed systems. Experienced in building production systems for LLM-based products, including agent frameworks, synthetic data generation pipelines, and evaluation platforms for reinforcement learning and model benchmarking. Background includes cloud infrastructure and AI platform development at Amazon and in a startup environment.

education.degree: Bachelor of Applied Science in Engineering

experience.title: Software Development Engineer (Amazon Ads)
bullets: Played a key role in the beta launch of an advertiser-facing AI agent that analyzes campaign performance data and generates actionable insights, improving advertisers' ability to plan and optimize campaigns.

Led development of an Ads AI agent framework on AWS Bedrock AgentCore and Strands SDK enabling multi-turn conversational agents with streaming responses, tool orchestration, MCP integrations, and memory persistence supporting short-term and long-term context with per-session and per-advertiser isolation.

Architected and built a distributed synthetic data generation platform that produced millions of high-quality LLM training samples across thousands of advertisers, enabling faster post-training workflows and overcoming cold-start constraints with multi-step ReAct reasoning traces and tool use via teacher models.

Developed a scalable LLM-as-Judge evaluation platform generating real-time reward signals for RL training and model evaluation pipelines, supporting high-throughput synchronous and asynchronous batch processing across thousands of evaluations.

experience.title: Software Development Engineer (Founding Team)
bullets: Collaboratively developed an AI-powered open source document processing framework, Sycamore, for RAG and unstructured analytics using Ray.
Led development of an MVP inference service using an in-house DETR model, enabling users to segment and partition PDFs efficiently.
Spearheaded the design and development of the control plane for the managed offering of the Sycamore platform, enabling the company to attract early adopters.
Regularly reviewed code and mentored junior developers, reinforcing engineering quality and continuous improvement.

experience.title: Software Development Engineer (Lake Formation & Glue Elastic Views)
bullets: Successfully launched a beta version of a managed service providing data storage optimization for consumer data lakes, improving efficiency and reducing cost.
Worked in a cross-functional environment to launch ACID transaction support in S3 data lakes.
Was an early team member of AWS Glue Elastic Views, a service that lets customers create materialized views across multiple data sources using familiar SQL without managing infrastructure.
Led the design and development of on-host TLS termination for two major services to achieve encryption in transit.
Collaborated with senior software engineers to design and develop a multi-tenant incremental view maintenance service using AWS technologies.

experience.title: Software Engineer Intern (Digital Content Production)
bullets: Assisted team leads and senior developers in creating the technical specs and architecture of a new digital publishing system.
Built and tested several microservice features with TypeScript, Node, and Kafka, reducing flyer generation time from 12 hours to about 1 hour.

experience.title: Research Intern (Computational Linguistics Lab)
bullets: Used NLP to determine cause of death from written descriptions of a person's symptoms leading up to death.
Created a machine learning pipeline in Python to investigate multiple algorithms and optimize classification accuracy.

publications.label: Co-authored "The Design of an LLM-powered Unstructured Analytics System" (arXiv:2409.00847) during my time at Aryn.ai for the Conference on Innovative Data Systems Research (CIDR).

posts.title: Designing ML platforms around real operational constraints
posts.page_title: Designing ML Platforms Around Constraints | Parth Parmar
posts.description: A blog post about using operational constraints to design better ML platforms.
posts.excerpt: Constraints around latency, tooling, and maintenance are often the shape of the actual platform problem.
posts.lead: The quality of an ML platform is usually determined less by its conceptual elegance than by how well it fits the constraints of the teams and services depending on it.

content.text: Platform work often goes wrong when teams optimize for theoretical flexibility instead of operational truth. A training platform may need strict conventions because the people using it are not platform specialists. An inference stack may need fewer extension points and better observability because paging frequency matters more than abstraction purity.

Constraints like latency budgets, GPU availability, run reproducibility, rollback safety, or artifact lineage are not secondary concerns. They define the system. If the platform ignores them, teams will route around it.

Strong platforms draw a line between what teams can self-serve and what the platform itself guarantees. That boundary should be shaped by support capacity and failure modes, not by a desire to look maximally general.

Most ML infrastructure lives in the boring middle: repeated runs, recurring incidents, changing schemas, and evolving models. Good platform design should make that steady state easier to manage rather than optimizing only for greenfield demos.

posts.title: What internal ML platforms should make easier
posts.page_title: What Internal ML Platforms Should Make Easier | Parth Parmar
posts.description: A blog post about evaluating the usefulness of internal ML platforms.
posts.excerpt: A practical test for platform work: does it reduce coordination cost and make delivery safer?
posts.lead: Platform teams should judge their work by whether they reduce coordination cost, shorten safe delivery paths, and improve the operating quality of model-backed systems.

content.text: Internal platforms can become warehouses for abstractions no one asked for. The better benchmark is simpler: does the platform make it easier to train, evaluate, deploy, observe, and roll back a model-backed change?

If product teams still rely on side channels, manual approvals, or undocumented recovery steps, the platform has not removed enough friction. A useful system should narrow the number of things teams need to remember and widen the guardrails that keep them safe.
Self-service is not the absence of control. In ML systems, it should mean common workflows are easy, validated, and observable, while exceptional workflows are intentionally harder.
Reliability is part of the platform interface
Teams feel platform quality through failure modes. Clear metrics, good rollout controls, dependency visibility, and obvious rollback paths often matter more than a larger feature surface.

posts.title: Improving inference systems without pausing delivery
posts.page_title: Improving Inference Systems Without Pausing Delivery | Parth Parmar
posts.description: A blog post about modernizing inference systems while continuing to ship product work.
posts.excerpt: A pragmatic approach to modernizing model-backed systems while continuing to ship product work.
posts.lead: Few teams can stop product work long enough to rebuild an inference stack. The more realistic approach is to improve the system at its highest-friction seams while traffic and feature delivery continue.

content.text: Model-backed services often accumulate fragile code around retries, fallback behavior, request shaping, or feature preparation. Those layers become risky when teams either avoid touching them or promise a full rewrite they cannot sustain.

A better method is to define a modernization policy. Improve observability first. Standardize how rollouts happen. Pull request traces and latency views closer to everyday engineering work. Then tighten one serving path at a time.
Whenever an inference-backed feature needs new behavior, use the change window to clean up adjacent system boundaries, remove special cases, and add minimal safety checks. Small upgrades compound.
Teams can live with old systems longer than they think if the systems are observable and controllable. Hidden failure modes are usually more dangerous than legacy code by itself.
